content.text: Log probabilities

(logprobs) are the logarithm of the probability the model assigned to each token at the moment it was generated. Every time a language model selects the next token, it first produces a probability distribution over its vocabulary; logprobs expose that distribution, token by token, to the calling application. Agents use logprobs to gauge model confidence, detect ambiguity, rerank multiple candidate completions, and build calibrated downstream decision logic.
What it is
For a token
generated at step
given all previous context
, the log-probability is:
Because probabilities are at most 1, log-probabilities are always
at most 0

. A value of 0 means the model was completely certain; a value of −10 means the model assigned roughly 1-in-22,026 odds. Most routine tokens in fluent text land in the range [−0.05, −1.5]; values below −3 typically indicate genuine uncertainty or out-of-distribution content.
APIs that expose logprobs usually return a
top_logprobs

field: a list of the N highest-probability tokens at each position, along with their log-probability values. This lets you see not just what the model chose, but what it was almost going to choose — a near-tie between two tokens at a decision point predicts unstable or paraphrase-diverse output.
Why it matters
Without logprobs, model output is an opaque string. With logprobs, each token carries a machine-readable confidence score. This unlocks several capabilities that prompting alone cannot provide:
Confidence gating: reject or re-sample completions that contain low-confidence tokens.
Reranking: generate N candidate completions and pick the one with the highest mean logprob.
Calibration measurement: compare model confidence against ground-truth accuracy across a dataset.
Uncertainty detection: flag responses where the model was genuinely uncertain (useful in RAG and classification tasks).
Perplexity scoring: evaluate how well the model fits a reference text, enabling quality measurement without human review.
Alignment debugging: identify exactly which token caused a format violation or unexpected turn in the output.
Benefits
No additional inference cost: logprobs are derived from the same forward pass that generates the token; they are free.
Granular signal: token-level confidence reveals exactly where the model hesitated, not just a sentence-level score.
Composable: logprobs can be aggregated, summed, averaged, or used as features in downstream ML models.
Model-agnostic metric: perplexity computed from logprobs is comparable across runs (same model, different prompts).
Useful for both streaming and batch: logprobs are returned per-token alongside content in streaming APIs.
Enable active learning: low-confidence tokens identify examples worth adding to fine-tuning datasets.
Trade-offs
Anthropic Claude does not expose logprobs: the Claude API returns only the generated text; you cannot access token-level probabilities.
Vocabulary-level granularity: logprobs reflect subword token probabilities, not word or phrase probabilities — aggregation requires care.
Top-logprobs list is capped: most APIs return at most 5–20 alternatives per position; the full distribution is not accessible.
Calibration varies by model: a high logprob does not always mean the answer is correct; different models have different calibration characteristics.
Increased response payload: returning logprobs for long completions can significantly increase the JSON response size.
Numerical precision: floating-point logprob values should not be compared across models without normalization.
How it works
After the softmax step, the model has a probability distribution
over the vocabulary. The log-probability is simply the natural logarithm of the probability of the selected token:
The log-probability of an entire sequence of T tokens is the sum of the per-token log-probabilities:

This additive property is why logprobs are preferable to raw probabilities for sequence scoring: multiplying T probabilities together causes numerical underflow for long sequences, while summing T log-probabilities stays numerically stable.
Perplexity is the exponential of the average per-token negative log-probability, and is widely used to measure how well a language model fits a body of text:
A perplexity of 10 means the model is, on average, as uncertain as if it were choosing uniformly among 10 equally likely tokens at each step. Lower perplexity indicates better fit.
Architecture

The diagram below shows a top-5 token ranking at a single generation step. The chosen token ("the") has a near-zero logprob, indicating high confidence. Subsequent candidates fall off sharply. A large gap between the first and second candidate indicates a confident, stable choice; a small gap indicates genuine ambiguity.

attrs.alt: Top-5 token candidates sorted by descending log-probability at a single generation step

content.text: The data flow during a single token generation step is:
Transformer forward pass over context tokens produces a logit vector z (shape: [vocab_size]).
Softmax converts z into probability distribution P.
Top-N probabilities are identified; log(P) is computed for each.
The selected token (argmax or sampled) is emitted along with its logprob.
The top_logprobs list (runner-up alternatives + their logprobs) is attached to the API response.
Token is appended to context; repeat from step 1.
When to use it
Good candidates
Classification tasks: comparing the logprob of 'Yes' vs 'No' tokens to make a binary decision rather than parsing natural language.
Reranking: generate 3–5 candidate completions with different seeds and select the one with the highest mean logprob.
Confidence thresholds: flag any response that contains a token with logprob below -3 as requiring human review.
Semantic similarity scoring: compare logprobs of a reference text under two different prompts to measure relevance.
Active dataset construction: mine production queries for low-confidence responses to use as fine-tuning examples.
Perplexity-based filtering: reject retrieved documents from RAG whose perplexity under the model is anomalously high.
Poor candidates
Open-ended creative generation: logprob-based reranking tends to prefer bland, high-frequency output over creative output.
Long reasoning chains: logprob aggregation across hundreds of tokens loses signal about specific decision points.
Cross-model comparison: logprobs are not comparable across different model families without calibration.
Anthropic Claude API: logprobs are not available; use alternative confidence signals instead.
How to implement
For OpenAI-compatible APIs, pass
logprobs=True
and optionally
top_logprobs=N
(1–20) in the request. The response includes a
logprobs
object with per-token entries.

# OpenAI-compatible API example (NOT Anthropic Claude)
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()  # Uses OPENAI_API_KEY env variable

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    max_tokens=64,
    logprobs=True,
    top_logprobs=5,  # Return top-5 alternatives per position
    messages=[
        {"role": "user", "content": "What is the capital of France?"}
    ]
)

for token_info in response.choices[0].logprobs.content:
    token = token_info.token
    lp = token_info.logprob
    prob = round(2.718281828 ** lp * 100, 2)  # Convert to percentage
    print(f"  Token: {repr(token):15s}  logprob: {lp:7.3f}  prob: {prob:5.1f}%")
    for alt in token_info.top_logprobs:
        if alt.token != token:
            alt_prob = round(2.718281828 ** alt.logprob * 100, 2)
            print(f"    alt: {repr(alt.token):12s}  logprob: {alt.logprob:7.3f}  prob: {alt_prob:5.1f}%")
Examples
Example 1: Confidence-gated response with Anthropic Claude
Because the Anthropic API does not expose token-level logprobs, you can build a proxy confidence measure using
multiple samples

. Generate N completions for the same prompt; if the model is confident, the outputs will be nearly identical (high agreement). If uncertain, they diverge. Agreement rate is a practical substitute for logprob-based confidence.

import anthropic
from collections import Counter

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def confidence_via_sampling(prompt: str, n_samples: int = 5) -> dict:
    """
    Approximate confidence by checking agreement across multiple samples.
    High agreement (same answer across most samples) = high confidence.
    Low agreement = low confidence / model is uncertain.

    This is the logprobs-alternative for Anthropic Claude.
    """
    responses = []
    for _ in range(n_samples):
        message = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5",  # Fast model for sampling
            max_tokens=8,
            temperature=0.5,
            system="Answer with a single word or number only. No explanation.",
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
        )
        answer = message.content[0].text.strip().lower()
        responses.append(answer)

    counts = Counter(responses)
    most_common, freq = counts.most_common(1)[0]
    agreement = freq / n_samples

    return {
        "answer": most_common,
        "confidence": agreement,
        "high_confidence": agreement >= 0.8,
        "all_responses": responses,
        "distribution": dict(counts),
    }

# Test on a confident question
result = confidence_via_sampling("What is 15 multiplied by 4?")
print(f"Answer: {result['answer']}")
print(f"Confidence: {result['confidence']:.0%} ({result['distribution']})")
print(f"High confidence: {result['high_confidence']}")
print()

# Test on an ambiguous question
result2 = confidence_via_sampling("Is Python or JavaScript more popular?")
print(f"Answer: {result2['answer']}")
print(f"Confidence: {result2['confidence']:.0%} ({result2['distribution']})")
print(f"High confidence: {result2['high_confidence']}")
Example 2: Reranking completions by sequence log-probability

This example shows how to score and rerank multiple candidate completions by computing an approximate sequence-level log-probability using the model's response agreement, then selecting the best candidate. For true logprob-based reranking, use an OpenAI-compatible API.

import anthropic
import math

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def generate_candidates(prompt: str, n: int = 4) -> list[str]:
    """Generate N diverse candidate completions."""
    candidates = []
    for _ in range(n):
        message = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=200,
            temperature=1.0,  # Higher temperature for diversity
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
        )
        candidates.append(message.content[0].text.strip())
    return candidates

def score_candidate(candidate: str, prompt: str) -> float:
    """
    Score a candidate by asking the model to rate its quality.
    Returns a score in [0, 1] as a proxy for sequence log-probability.
    In a real system with logprob support, use the sum of token logprobs instead.
    """
    message = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",
        max_tokens=8,
        system="Rate the quality of this response on a scale from 0 to 10. Reply with a single integer only.",
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "user",
                "content": f"Question: {prompt}\n\nResponse to rate: {candidate}"
            }
        ]
    )
    try:
        score = int(message.content[0].text.strip())
        return score / 10.0
    except ValueError:
        return 0.5

def rerank_completions(prompt: str) -> dict:
    """Generate candidates, score each, return the best one."""
    print(f"Generating candidates for: {prompt[:60]}...")
    candidates = generate_candidates(prompt, n=3)

    scored = []
    for i, candidate in enumerate(candidates):
        score = score_candidate(candidate, prompt)
        scored.append({"candidate": candidate, "score": score, "rank": i})
        print(f"  Candidate {i+1}: score={score:.2f}  ({candidate[:60]}...)")

    best = max(scored, key=lambda x: x["score"])
    return {
        "best_candidate": best["candidate"],
        "score": best["score"],
        "all_scored": scored,
    }

result = rerank_completions(
    "Explain the difference between a list and a tuple in Python in one sentence."
)
print(f"\nBest answer (score={result['score']:.2f}):")
print(result["best_candidate"])
Real-world example

A legal document review system uses logprobs (via an OpenAI-compatible model) to flag uncertain entity extractions. For each extracted entity (company name, date, clause type), the system checks whether the logprob of the first token in the extracted span falls below a threshold of −1.5. Any extraction below the threshold is tagged with a "needs review" annotation rather than being committed to the database automatically. This reduces the false-positive extraction rate from 12% to under 3% without requiring a separate classifier model.
Common mistakes
Treating logprobs as absolute quality scores: a high logprob means the model was confident, not that it was correct.
Comparing logprobs across models: probabilities are calibrated differently across model families; comparison is meaningless without normalization.
Using average logprob for long sequences: the mean is dominated by filler tokens (articles, punctuation) which are always high-confidence; use minimum logprob or focus on content tokens.
Ignoring tokenization when computing perplexity: whitespace and punctuation tokens inflate the token count; normalize by word count for comparable perplexity values.
Assuming low logprob = wrong: the model may have been uncertain between two equally correct paraphrases; low confidence does not imply factual error.
Requesting top_logprobs on every token in long documents: this dramatically increases response payload size; use it selectively on decision-making tokens only.
Best practices
Use minimum token logprob (not mean) to detect worst-case uncertainty in a span.
Calibrate your threshold on a held-out validation set; do not use -1.5 as a universal threshold without empirical support.
For classification: extract the logprob of the class-label token (e.g., 'Yes', 'No', 'A', 'B') rather than parsing the full response.
Normalize sequence logprobs by token count when comparing completions of different lengths.
Log and monitor logprob distributions in production to detect model drift or prompt degradation over time.
For Anthropic Claude: use multi-sample agreement as a confidence proxy and structured outputs via tool use to constrain vocabulary.
Combine logprob thresholds with human-review queues: automatically approve high-confidence outputs, escalate low-confidence ones.
When fine-tuning, weight training examples by their baseline perplexity to prioritize hard examples.
Performance & scalability
Requesting logprobs has negligible per-token computational cost since the probability distribution is computed as part of every forward pass. However, there are system-level considerations:
Response size: a top_logprobs=5 request for a 200-token completion adds roughly 5x the token metadata to the JSON payload, increasing bandwidth and parsing time.
Sampling overhead: reranking over N candidates multiplies API cost by N; use a cheaper model for the scoring step.
Streaming compatibility: logprobs can be streamed per-token alongside content in OpenAI's streaming API, enabling real-time confidence dashboards.
Caching: responses that include logprobs cannot always be cached by prompt-cache infrastructure since the logprob metadata is request-specific.
Tooling & ecosystem
OpenAI Python SDK: logprobs=True parameter on chat.completions.create; returns ChatCompletionTokenLogprob objects.
vLLM: logprobs parameter on the /v1/completions endpoint; production-grade serving with logprob support.
lm-eval-harness (EleutherAI): uses logprobs for zero-shot and few-shot benchmark evaluation across models.
Guidance (Microsoft): uses logprobs internally for constrained generation and interleaved control flow.
LangChain: some LLM wrappers expose logprobs via generation_info; check model-specific docs.
Anthropic Claude SDK: no logprobs exposure; use multi-sample agreement pattern shown in Example 1.

Security & reliability
Logprobs expose the model's internal confidence, which creates two security considerations in agentic systems:

Adversarial probing: an attacker who can query the API may use logprob responses to probe which tokens the model assigns high probability to, potentially extracting fine-tuning data or system prompt fragments through membership inference attacks.
Confidence inflation: a model that has been fine-tuned to be overconfident (high logprobs for wrong answers) can fool logprob-based gates. Always calibrate thresholds on your specific model version.
Privacy leakage: logprobs can reveal which user-supplied tokens the model found surprising (low logprob) vs. familiar (high logprob), which may leak information about training data composition.
For reliability, treat logprob thresholds as
heuristics

, not guarantees. A token with logprob −0.1 can still be wrong; a token with logprob −4.0 can still be correct. The signal is probabilistic and must be combined with ground-truth validation in production pipelines.
Mental model
Think of logprobs as
the model's own uncertainty report

, delivered token by token. When you read a sentence and a word feels unexpected, your brain assigns it low probability and your reading slows. Logprobs expose the model's equivalent experience: low logprob at a token means the model was surprised — the context did not strongly predict that word. High logprob means the token was nearly inevitable given what came before.
The top_logprobs list at a position is the model's set of
nearest alternatives

. A very flat top-5 distribution (all values within 0.5 of each other) signals genuine semantic ambiguity at that position. A very sharp distribution (first value near 0, rest below −3) signals high-confidence, stable output. Reading this landscape lets you build systems that act differently depending on whether the model is sure or guessing.
Key takeaways
Log-probability is the natural log of the probability the model assigned to the token it generated; values are always <= 0.
Sequence log-probability is the sum of per-token logprobs; this is numerically stable and proportional to the model's confidence in the entire sequence.
Perplexity (exp of mean negative logprob) is the standard benchmark metric for language model fit; lower is better.
Anthropic Claude does not expose logprobs; approximate confidence with multi-sample agreement or use an OpenAI-compatible model for logprob-dependent pipelines.
Use minimum token logprob (not mean) to flag worst-case uncertainty in a completion.
Top-logprobs lists expose near-ties that predict ambiguous, paraphrase-diverse, or unstable output.
Always calibrate logprob thresholds empirically; -1.5 is not a universal boundary between confident and uncertain.
Combine logprob gating with human review queues rather than using it as a binary accept/reject filter.
